title: Shift-weighted specialist blend with final isotonic mapping
summary: Tabular OOD work shows robustness to distribution shift is partly separate from in-domain fit.
rationale: Tabular OOD work shows robustness to distribution shift is partly separate from in-domain fit. This competition mixes seasons, circuits, and pre-season testing, so a shift-aware blend of specialists may travel better than a single global model. A final monotone calibrator can improve probability quality while preserving the ranking structure that drives ROC AUC.
implementation_hint: Train a few diverse specialists, for example dry-slick vs wet/mixed, early-race vs late-race, and CatBoost vs standard GBDT. Estimate train-to-test similarity with a domain classifier or density-ratio model on covariates like `Year`, `Race`, `Compound`, `RaceProgress`, and `Stint`, then use those weights when learning the OOF blend. Fit one isotonic regressor on blended OOF scores only before generating test probabilities.
expected_effect: Should improve leaderboard robustness if the hidden test mix differs from CV, while keeping the final prediction map AUC-friendly.
risk: Importance weighting can add variance and hurt local CV; too many specialists can waste data and create unstable blend weights.
